# p24p Runtime Library

The runtime library provides execution-time services for Pascal programs compiled by p24p. It is written in Pascal, compiled by p24p itself into .spc, assembled by pasm into .p24 bytecode, and loaded alongside user programs on the pv24a VM.

## Architecture

```
runtime.pas ──→ p24p (C compiler) ──→ runtime.spc
                                          │
user.pas ───→ p24p (C compiler) ───→ user.spc
                                          │
                              ┌───────────┘
                              ▼
                     pasm (assembler)
                              │
                              ▼
                     combined .p24 bytecode
                              │
                              ▼
                     pv24a VM (execution)
                              │
                              ▼
                     web-dv24r (Rust/Yew debugger)
```

The compiler emits `call` instructions to runtime routines. pasm resolves these references when assembling the combined .spc output (runtime + user program) into a single .p24 file.

## Linking Model

Phase 0 uses a simple concatenation model: the compiler's .spc output is appended after the runtime .spc stubs. pasm sees all procedures in one assembly unit and resolves all `call` targets by name.

```
; runtime.spc (always included)
.proc _p24p_write_int 0
    ...
.end

.proc _p24p_write_bool 0
    ...
.end

.proc _p24p_write_ln 0
    ...
.end

; user program .spc (compiler output)
.proc main 0
    enter 0
    push 42
    call _p24p_write_int
    call _p24p_write_ln
    halt
.end
```

## Runtime Components by Phase

### Phase 0 — Hand-Written .spc (Permanent)

These routines wrap `sys` instructions directly and stay as hand-written .spc forever. There is no benefit to rewriting them in Pascal since they are thin wrappers around VM syscalls.

| Routine | Stack Effect | Description |
|---------|-------------|-------------|
| `_p24p_write_int` | ( n -- ) | Print signed integer to UART as decimal |
| `_p24p_write_bool` | ( b -- ) | Print `TRUE` or `FALSE` to UART |
| `_p24p_write_ln` | ( -- ) | Print newline (LF) to UART |

**Implementation notes:**
- `_p24p_write_int` handles sign, converts to decimal digits via repeated div/mod, outputs via `sys 1` (PUTC)
- `_p24p_write_bool` compares to 0, branches to print "TRUE" or "FALSE" character by character
- `_p24p_write_ln` pushes 10 (LF) and calls `sys 1`

### Phase 1 — Pascal Runtime (Dogfooding Begins)

Once the compiler supports procedures, functions, arrays, records, and characters, these routines can be written in Pascal and compiled by p24p itself.

| Routine | Description | Compiler Features Exercised |
|---------|-------------|---------------------------|
| `abs(x)` | Absolute value | functions, if/then/else |
| `odd(x)` | Test if odd | functions, mod |
| `ord(c)` | Character to integer | functions, char type |
| `chr(n)` | Integer to character | functions, char type |
| `succ(x)` | Next ordinal value | functions, arithmetic |
| `pred(x)` | Previous ordinal value | functions, arithmetic |
| `sqr(x)` | Square of integer | functions, multiplication |
| `_p24p_bounds_check` | Array bounds violation handler | procedures, writeln, arrays |
| `_p24p_nil_check` | Nil pointer dereference handler | procedures, pointers |

Each routine tests the compiler feature that enables it — this is the dogfooding feedback loop.

### Phase 1 — Read/Readln Support

| Routine | Stack Effect | Description |
|---------|-------------|-------------|
| `_p24p_read_int` | ( -- n ) | Read integer from UART (sign + digits) |
| `_p24p_read_char` | ( -- c ) | Read single character from UART |
| `_p24p_read_ln` | ( -- ) | Skip to next line (consume through LF) |

These use `sys 2` (GETC) internally. The integer reader handles optional sign and digit accumulation. Written in Pascal once char type and loops are available.

### Phase 2 — Heap and Dynamic Features (Pascal)

| Routine | Description | Compiler Features Exercised |
|---------|-------------|---------------------------|
| `_p24p_heap_init` | Initialize free list | pointers, records, globals |
| `_p24p_new(size)` | Allocate heap block | pointers, records, sys ALLOC |
| `_p24p_dispose(ptr)` | Free heap block | pointers, records, sys FREE |
| `_p24p_leak_report` | Report unfreed allocations at exit | arrays, records, writeln |
| `_p24p_set_union` | Set union (bit-vector OR) | arrays, bitwise ops |
| `_p24p_set_intersect` | Set intersection (bit-vector AND) | arrays, bitwise ops |
| `_p24p_set_diff` | Set difference | arrays, bitwise ops |
| `_p24p_set_member` | Set membership test | arrays, bitwise ops |
| `_p24p_str_compare` | String comparison | arrays, char type, loops |
| `_p24p_str_copy` | String copy | arrays, char type |
| `_p24p_str_concat` | String concatenation | arrays, char type, new |
| `_p24p_subrange_check` | Subrange violation handler | procedures, writeln |

The heap allocator is the flagship dogfooding target — it exercises pointers, records, arrays, and the VM's memory model simultaneously.

## VM Syscall Interface

The runtime communicates with the VM through `sys` instructions:

| ID | Name | Stack Effect | Description |
|----|------|-------------|-------------|
| 0 | HALT | ( -- ) | Stop execution |
| 1 | PUTC | ( c -- ) | Write byte to UART |
| 2 | GETC | ( -- c ) | Read byte from UART (blocking) |
| 3 | LED | ( n -- ) | Write LED state |
| 4 | ALLOC | ( size -- addr ) | Heap allocate |
| 5 | FREE | ( addr -- ) | Heap free |

Phase 0 runtime uses only PUTC. Phase 1 adds GETC. Phase 2 adds ALLOC/FREE.

## Debugging with web-dv24r

The web-dv24r browser debugger (Rust/Yew/WASM) provides p-code-level visibility into runtime execution:

- **Step through runtime code** at the p-code instruction level — see each `push`, `div`, `mod`, `sys 1` as `_p24p_write_int` converts a number to decimal digits
- **Eval stack inspection** — watch values flow through runtime routines
- **Call frame panel** — see the call chain from user code into runtime procedures
- **Memory map** — inspect globals, heap state, and VM regions
- **Delta highlighting** — see what changed after each instruction

This makes the runtime fully transparent during development. When a runtime routine misbehaves, the debugger shows exactly which p-code instruction produced the wrong result — no printf debugging needed.

## Naming Convention

All runtime routines use the `_p24p_` prefix to avoid collisions with user-defined Pascal identifiers. Standard functions (`abs`, `odd`, etc.) are exposed under their Pascal names but implemented as calls to prefixed internal routines where needed.

1→# pv24a — Design Document
     2→
     3→## 1. VM State
     4→
     5→The p-code VM maintains the following state in a memory struct:
     6→
     7→```
     8→vm_state:
     9→    .word 0     ; pc   — p-code instruction pointer
    10→    .word 0     ; esp  — eval stack pointer (top of eval stack)
    11→    .word 0     ; csp  — call stack pointer (top of call stack)
    12→    .word 0     ; fp   — current frame pointer (call stack)
    13→    .word 0     ; gp   — globals base address
    14→    .word 0     ; hp   — heap pointer (next free)
    15→    .word 0     ; code — base address of p-code segment
    16→    .word 0     ; status — 0=running, 1=halted, 2=trapped
    17→    .word 0     ; trap_code — last trap reason
    18→```
    19→
    20→## 2. P-Code Instruction Set
    21→
    22→### 2.1 Stack / Constants
    23→
    24→| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
    25→|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
    26→| `push` imm24 | 4 bytes | ( -- n ) | Push 24-bit immediate |
    27→| `push_s` imm8 | 2 bytes | ( -- n ) | Push sign-extended 8-bit immediate |
    28→| `dup` | 1 byte | ( a -- a a ) | Duplicate top |
    29→| `drop` | 1 byte | ( a -- ) | Discard top |
    30→| `swap` | 1 byte | ( a b -- b a ) | Swap top two |
    31→| `over` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a b a ) | Copy second to top |
    32→
    33→### 2.2 Arithmetic / Logic
    34→
    35→| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
    36→|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
    37→| `add` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a+b ) | Add |
    38→| `sub` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a-b ) | Subtract |
    39→| `mul` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a*b ) | Multiply |
    40→| `div` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a/b ) | Divide (trap on b=0) |
    41→| `mod` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a%b ) | Modulo (trap on b=0) |
    42→| `neg` | 1 byte | ( a -- -a ) | Negate |
    43→| `and` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a&b ) | Bitwise AND |
    44→| `or` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a\|b ) | Bitwise OR |
    45→| `xor` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a^b ) | Bitwise XOR |
    46→| `not` | 1 byte | ( a -- ~a ) | Bitwise NOT |
    47→| `shl` | 1 byte | ( a n -- a<<n ) | Shift left |
    48→| `shr` | 1 byte | ( a n -- a>>n ) | Arithmetic shift right |
    49→
    50→### 2.3 Comparison
    51→
    52→All comparisons push 1 (true) or 0 (false):
    53→
    54→| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
    55→|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
    56→| `eq` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Equal |
    57→| `ne` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Not equal |
    58→| `lt` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Less than (signed) |
    59→| `le` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Less or equal (signed) |
    60→| `gt` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Greater than (signed) |
    61→| `ge` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Greater or equal (signed) |
    62→
    63→### 2.4 Control Flow
    64→
    65→| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
    66→|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
    67→| `jmp` addr24 | 4 bytes | ( -- ) | Unconditional jump |
    68→| `jz` addr24 | 4 bytes | ( flag -- ) | Jump if zero |
    69→| `jnz` addr24 | 4 bytes | ( flag -- ) | Jump if nonzero |
    70→| `call` addr24 | 4 bytes | ( args... -- ) | Call procedure (static link = 0) |
    71→| `calln` depth8 addr24 | 5 bytes | ( args... -- ) | Call with static link (depth=0: nested, depth=N: N levels up) |
    72→| `ret` nargs8 | 2 bytes | ( [retval] -- [retval] ) | Return, clean nargs |
    73→| `halt` | 1 byte | ( -- ) | Stop VM |
    74→| `trap` code8 | 2 bytes | ( -- ) | Trigger trap |

    88→| `loadn` depth8 off8 | 3 bytes | ( -- val ) | Load nonlocal via static link chain |

   124→## 3. Call Frame Layout
   125→
   126→When `call` executes, a new frame is pushed onto the call stack:
   127→
   128→```
   129→         ┌──────────────────┐  ← new csp (after enter)
   130→         │ local[N-1]       │  offset = +(N-1)*3 from fp
   131→         │ ...              │
   132→         │ local[0]         │  offset = 0 from fp
   133→fp ───→  ├──────────────────┤
   134→         │ procedure ID     │  offset = -3
   135→         │ static link      │  offset = -6  (lexically enclosing frame)
   136→         │ dynamic link     │  offset = -9  (caller's fp)
   137→         │ return PC        │  offset = -12
   138→         ├──────────────────┤
   139→         │ arg[N-1]         │  offset = -15  (from fp)
   140→         │ ...              │
   141→         │ arg[0]           │  offset = -15 - (N-1)*3
   142→         └──────────────────┘
   143→```
   144→
   145→- **Caller** pushes arguments onto eval stack, then `call addr`
   146→- **call** instruction: pops args from eval stack into call stack frame header,
   147→  pushes return PC, dynamic link, static link, proc ID
   148→- **enter N**: advances csp by N*3 to reserve local slots, sets fp
   149→- **leave**: restores fp to dynamic link, restores csp
   150→- **ret N**: pushes return value (if any) to eval stack, pops frame, cleans N args
   151→
   152→## 4. Calling Convention
   153→
   154→Simple and explicit:
   155→
   156→1. Caller pushes arguments left-to-right onto eval stack
   157→2. Caller executes `call <proc_addr>`
   158→3. Callee executes `enter <nlocals>` (reserves local slots)
   159→4. Callee accesses args via `loada 0`, `loada 1`, etc.
   160→5. Callee accesses locals via `loadl 0`, `loadl 1`, etc.
   161→6. For functions: callee pushes return value onto eval stack
   162→7. Callee executes `ret <nargs>` (cleans args from frame)
   163→8. Caller finds return value (if any) on eval stack
   164→
   165→## 5. P-Code Assembler (pasm) Syntax
   166→
   167→### 5.0 File Extensions
   168→
   169→- `.spc` — p-code assembler source files (input to pasm)
   170→- `.p24` — assembled p-code bytecode files (output of pasm, input to VM)
   171→
   172→### 5.1 Source Format
   173→
   174→```
   175→; Comment (semicolon to end of line)
   176→
   177→.const UART_DATA  -65280      ; named constant
   178→.const UART_STATUS -65279
   179→.const LED_PORT   -65024
   180→
   181→.global counter 1             ; 1 word of global storage
   182→.global buffer 32             ; 32 words
   183→
   184→.data msg 72, 101, 108, 108, 111, 0   ; "Hello\0" as bytes
   185→
   186→.proc main 0                 ; procedure, 0 locals
   187→    push msg                  ; push address of msg
   188→    call puts
   189→    halt
   190→.end
   191→
   192→.proc puts 1                 ; 1 local (loop pointer)
   193→    loada 0                   ; arg0 = string address
   194→    storel 0                  ; local0 = current ptr
   195→loop:
   196→    loadl 0
   197→    loadb                     ; load byte at ptr
   198→    dup
   199→    jz done                   ; if zero, done
   200→    sys 1                     ; PUTC
   201→    loadl 0
   202→    push 1
   203→    add
   204→    storel 0                  ; ptr++
   205→    jmp loop
   206→done:
   207→    drop                      ; drop the zero
   208→    ret 1                     ; return, clean 1 arg
   209→.end
   210→```
   211→
   212→### 5.2 Directives
   213→
   214→| Directive | Syntax | Description |
   215→|-----------|--------|-------------|
   216→| `.const` | `.const NAME value` | Define named constant |
   217→| `.global` | `.global NAME nwords` | Reserve global storage |
   218→| `.data` | `.data NAME byte, byte, ...` | Define byte data (string/array) |
   219→| `.proc` | `.proc NAME nlocals` | Begin procedure (nlocals = local slot count) |
   220→| `.end` | `.end` | End procedure |
   221→
   222→### 5.3 Labels
   223→
   224→Labels appear on their own line, ending with `:`:
   225→
   226→```
   227→loop:
   228→    ; instructions...
   229→    jmp loop
   230→```
   231→
   232→Labels are local to the enclosing `.proc` / `.end` block.
   233→
   234→### 5.4 Integer Literals
   235→
   236→- Decimal: `42`, `-1`, `0`
   237→- Hex: `0xFF` (if supported) or decimal equivalents
   238→- Named constants: `UART_DATA` (substituted by assembler)
   239→
   240→### 5.5 Instructions
   241→
   242→One instruction per line. Operand follows mnemonic separated by whitespace:
   243→
   244→```
   245→push 42
   246→loadl 0
   247→jz done
   248→call puts
   249→sys 1
   250→ret 0
   251→```

   311→## 7. Trap Codes
   312→
   313→| Code | Name | Trigger |
   314→|------|------|---------|
   315→| 0 | USER_TRAP | `trap 0` instruction |
   316→| 1 | DIV_ZERO | Division/modulo by zero |

   326→## 8. Memory Segment Layout
   327→
   328→At VM initialization:
   329→
   330→1. **Code segment** starts at a fixed base address (after VM code)
   331→2. **Constants/data** follows code (emitted by pasm)
   332→3. **Globals** follow data (allocated per `.global` directives)
   333→4. **Call stack** starts after globals, grows upward
   334→5. **Eval stack** starts after call stack region, grows upward
   335→6. **Heap** starts after eval stack region, grows upward
   336→7. Segment base addresses stored in VM state struct
   337→
   338→Sizes are configurable at VM init time. Default allocations:
   339→- Call stack: 4 KB
   340→- Eval stack: 2 KB
   341→- Heap: remaining SRAM up to ~900 KB

   376→## 10. COR24 Assembly Patterns
   377→
   378→Reference patterns from tf24a for common idioms:
   379→
   380→### Dispatch Table
   381→```asm
   382→; Index into jump table by opcode byte
   383→; r0 = opcode
   384→la r2, dispatch_table
   385→; multiply r0 by 3 (entry size = .word = 3 bytes)
   386→; ... load target address, jmp
   387→```
   388→
   389→### UART Output
   390→```asm
   391→; r0 = character to send

1→; pasm.s — P-Code Assembler for COR24
     2→;
     3→; Two-pass assembler: reads .spc source from UART, assembles to bytecode.
     4→;   Pass 1: collect symbols (labels, procs, consts, globals, data), compute sizes
     5→;   Pass 2: emit bytecode, resolve symbol references
     6→;
     7→; Token types:
     8→;   0 = TOK_EOF     end of input
     9→;   1 = TOK_NL      newline
    10→;   2 = TOK_NUM     integer literal (value in tok_value)
    11→;   3 = TOK_IDENT   identifier/mnemonic/name (string in tok_buf)
    12→;   4 = TOK_DIR     directive without '.' (string in tok_buf)
    13→;   5 = TOK_LABEL   label definition without ':' (string in tok_buf)
    14→;   6 = TOK_COMMA   comma separator
    15→;
    16→; Symbol types:
    17→;   0 = SYM_CONST   named constant (value = literal)
    18→;   1 = SYM_LABEL   code label (value = code offset)
    19→;   2 = SYM_PROC    procedure entry (value = code offset)
    20→;   3 = SYM_GLOBAL  global variable (value = global segment offset)
    21→;   4 = SYM_DATA    data block (value = data segment offset)
    22→;
    23→; Operand types (mnemonic table):
    24→;   0 = NONE    1-byte instruction
    25→;   1 = IMM8    2-byte: opcode + byte
    26→;   2 = IMM24   4-byte: opcode + word
    27→;   3 = D8_A24  5-byte: opcode + byte + word (calln)
    28→;   4 = D8_O8   3-byte: opcode + byte + byte (loadn/storen)
    29→;
    30→; Register allocation:
    31→;   r0 = work/scratch, parameter, return value
    32→;   r1 = return address (jal) or scratch
    33→;   r2 = scratch, function address for jal
    34→;   fp = memory base for indexed loads/stores
    35→;   sp = COR24 hardware stack (EBR)
    36→;
    37→; UART: data at -65280 (0xFF0100), status at -65279 (0xFF0101)
    38→;   TX busy = status bit 7 (sign bit via lb sign-extend)
    39→;   RX ready = status bit 0
    40→
    41→; ============================================================
    42→; Entry point
    43→; ============================================================
    44→_start:
    45→    ; Print boot message
    46→    la r0, msg_boot
    47→    la r2, uart_puts
    48→    jal r1, (r2)
    49→
    50→    ; Read all UART input into input_buf
    51→    la r2, read_all_input
    52→    jal r1, (r2)
    53→
    54→    ; ---- Pass 1: collect symbols, compute sizes ----
    55→    lc r0, 0
    56→    la r2, input_pos
    57→    sw r0, 0(r2)
    58→    ; Prime first character
    59→    la r2, lex_advance
    60→    jal r1, (r2)
    61→    ; Set pass number = 1
    62→    lc r0, 1
    63→    la r2, pass_num
    64→    sb r0, 0(r2)
    65→    ; Reset counters
    66→    lc r0, 0
    67→    la r2, code_addr
    68→    sw r0, 0(r2)
    69→    la r2, global_offset
    70→    sw r0, 0(r2)
    71→    la r2, data_offset
    72→    sw r0, 0(r2)
    73→    la r2, sym_count
    74→    sw r0, 0(r2)
    75→    ; Initialize name_pool_ptr
    76→    la r0, name_pool
    77→    la r2, name_pool_ptr
    78→    sw r0, 0(r2)
    79→    ; Run pass 1
    80→    la r2, parse_program
    81→    jal r1, (r2)
    82→
    83→    ; Save code size after pass 1
    84→    la r2, code_addr
    85→    lw r0, 0(r2)
    86→    la r2, code_size
    87→    sw r0, 0(r2)
    88→    ; Save data size
    89→    la r2, data_offset
    90→    lw r0, 0(r2)
    91→    la r2, total_data_size
    92→    sw r0, 0(r2)
    93→
    94→    ; Patch data and global symbols with absolute offsets
    95→    la r2, patch_symbols
    96→    jal r1, (r2)
    97→
    98→    ; ---- Pass 2: emit bytecode ----
    99→    lc r0, 0
   100→    la r2, input_pos

   102→    ; Prime first character
   103→    la r2, lex_advance
   104→    jal r1, (r2)
   105→    ; Set pass number = 2
   106→    lc r0, 2
   107→    la r2, pass_num

   113→    ; Initialize data output pointer

   115→    la r2, data_ptr

   120→

   136→; uart_putc — send byte in r0 to UART
   137→; Leaf function. Clobbers: r0, r2. Preserves: r1.
   138→uart_putc:

   141→uart_putc_wait:

   143→    cls r0, z
   144→    brt uart_putc_wait

   146→    sb r0, 0(r2)
   147→    jmp (r1)

   154→uart_puts_loop:

   170→uart_puts_done:

   173→
   174→; ============================================================
   175→; read_all_input — read UART into input_buf until EOT (0x04)
   176→; ============================================================

   178→read_all_input:

1→; pvm.s — P-Code Virtual Machine for COR24
     2→;
     3→; Register allocation:
     4→;   r0 = W (work/scratch, opcode dispatch)
     5→;   r1 = scratch / return address for jal calls
     6→;   r2 = scratch
     7→;   sp = COR24 hardware stack (EBR, used sparingly)
     8→;   fp = memory base for indexed loads/stores
     9→;
    10→; VM state lives in memory struct (vm_state) because COR24 has only 3 GPRs.
    11→;
    12→; UART: data at -65280 (0xFF0100), status at -65279 (0xFF0101)
    13→;   TX busy = status bit 7 (sign bit via lb sign-extend)
    14→;   RX ready = status bit 0
    15→; LED: port at -65024 (0xFF0200)
    16→;
    17→; COR24 ISA notes:
    18→;   lbu = load byte zero-extend, lb = load byte sign-extend
    19→;   ceq ra, rb sets C if ra == rb; cls ra, rb sets C if ra < rb (signed)
    20→;   clu ra, rb sets C if ra < rb (unsigned)
    21→;   brt/brf = branch if C true/false; bra = branch always
    22→;   jal r1, (r2) = r1 = PC+1, PC = r2 (call convention)
    23→;   jmp (r1) = return from jal call
    24→;   Valid load/store base registers: r0, r1, r2, fp (NOT sp)
    25→;   Can push/pop: r0, r1, r2, fp
    26→
    27→; ============================================================
    28→; Entry point
    29→; ============================================================
    30→_start:
    31→    ; Initialize VM state struct (fp = &vm_state)
    32→    la r0, vm_state
    33→    push r0
    34→    pop fp
    35→
    36→    ; pc = 0
    37→    lc r0, 0
    38→    sw r0, 0(fp)
    39→
    40→    ; esp = eval_stack base
    41→    la r0, eval_stack
    42→    sw r0, 3(fp)
    43→
    44→    ; csp = call_stack base
    45→    la r0, call_stack
    46→    sw r0, 6(fp)
    47→
    48→    ; fp_vm = 0 (no frame yet)
    49→    lc r0, 0
    50→    sw r0, 9(fp)
    51→
    52→    ; gp = globals base
    53→    la r0, globals_seg
    54→    sw r0, 12(fp)
    55→
    56→    ; hp = heap base
    57→    la r0, heap_seg
    58→    sw r0, 15(fp)
    59→
    60→    ; code = code segment base
    61→    la r0, code_seg
    62→    sw r0, 18(fp)
    63→
    64→    ; status = 0 (running)
    65→    lc r0, 0
    66→    sw r0, 21(fp)
    67→
    68→    ; trap_code = 0
    69→    lc r0, 0
    70→    sw r0, 24(fp)
    71→
    72→    ; Print boot message
    73→    la r0, msg_boot
    74→    la r2, uart_puts
    75→    jal r1, (r2)
    76→
    77→    ; Enter VM main loop
    78→    la r0, vm_loop
    79→    jmp (r0)
    80→
    81→; ============================================================
    82→; UART helpers
    83→; ============================================================
    84→
    85→; uart_putc — send byte in r0 to UART
    86→; Call via: jal r1, (r2) with r2 = uart_putc
    87→; Clobbers: r0, r2 (r1 = return address, preserved)
    88→uart_putc:
    89→    push r0
    90→    la r2, -65280
    91→uart_putc_wait:
    92→    lb r0, 1(r2)
    93→    cls r0, z
    94→    brt uart_putc_wait
    95→    pop r0
    96→    sb r0, 0(r2)
    97→    jmp (r1)
    98→
    99→; uart_puts — print null-terminated string at address in r0
   100→; Call via: jal r1, (r2) with r2 = uart_puts, r0 = string addr
   101→; Clobbers: r0, r1, r2
   102→uart_puts:
   103→    push r1
   104→    mov r1, r0
   105→uart_puts_loop:
   106→    lbu r0, 0(r1)
   107→    ceq r0, z
   108→    brt uart_puts_done
   109→    push r1
   110→    push r0
   111→    la r2, -65280
   112→uart_puts_tx:
   113→    lb r0, 1(r2)
   114→    cls r0, z
   115→    brt uart_puts_tx
   116→    pop r0
   117→    sb r0, 0(r2)
   118→    pop r1
   119→    add r1, 1
   120→    bra uart_puts_loop
   121→uart_puts_done:
   122→    pop r1
   123→    jmp (r1)
   124→
   125→; ============================================================
   126→; VM fetch-decode-execute loop
   127→; ============================================================
   128→vm_loop:
   129→    ; Check status: if not running (0), stop
   130→    la r0, vm_state
   131→    push r0
   132→    pop fp
   133→    lw r0, 21(fp)
   134→    ceq r0, z
   135→    brf vm_halted
   136→
   137→    ; Fetch opcode byte from code[pc]
   138→    lw r0, 18(fp)
   139→    lw r2, 0(fp)
   140→    add r0, r2
   141→    ; r0 = code_base + pc, use r0 as base register
   142→    lbu r2, 0(r0)
   143→    ; r2 = opcode byte (zero-extended)
   144→
   145→    ; Increment pc
   146→    lw r0, 0(fp)
   147→    add r0, 1
   148→    sw r0, 0(fp)
   149→
   150→    ; Bounds check: opcode must be < 97 (0x00..0x60)
   151→    mov r0, r2
   152→    lc r2, 97
   153→    clu r0, r2
   154→    brt opcode_ok
   155→    la r0, op_invalid
   156→    jmp (r0)
   157→opcode_ok:
   158→
   159→    ; Dispatch: compute dispatch_table[opcode * 3]
   160→    mov r2, r0
   161→    add r0, r0
   162→    add r0, r2
   163→    ; r0 = 3 * opcode
   164→    la r2, dispatch_table
   165→    add r2, r0
   166→    ; r2 = &dispatch_table[opcode * 3]
   167→    lw r0, 0(r2)
   168→    ; r0 = handler address
   169→    jmp (r0)
   170→
   171→; ============================================================
   172→; VM halt / trap exit
   173→; ============================================================
   174→vm_halted:
   175→    ; r0 = status (nonzero)
   176→    ; Check if trapped (status == 2)
   177→    lc r2, 2
   178→    ceq r0, r2
   179→    brt vm_trapped
   180→    ; Normal halt
   181→    la r0, msg_halted
   182→    la r2, uart_puts_final
   183→    jal r1, (r2)
   184→vm_trapped:
   185→    ; Print "TRAP " prefix (uart_puts preserves r1)
   186→    la r0, msg_trap_prefix
   187→    la r2, uart_puts
   188→    jal r1, (r2)
   189→    ; Load trap_code from vm_state
   190→    la r0, vm_state
   191→    push r0
   192→    pop fp
   193→    lw r0, 24(fp)
   194→    ; Convert to ASCII digit (codes 0-7)
   195→    add r0, 48
   196→    ; Print digit via uart_putc
   197→    la r2, uart_putc
   198→    jal r1, (r2)
   199→    ; Print newline
   200→    lc r0, 10
   201→    la r2, uart_putc
   202→    jal r1, (r2)
   203→    ; Halt
   204→    la r0, halt_loop
   205→    jmp (r0)
   206→
   207→; uart_puts_final — print string then enter halt loop
   208→; r0 = string address. Does not return.
   209→uart_puts_final:
   210→    mov r1, r0
   211→uart_puts_final_loop:
   212→    lbu r0, 0(r1)
   213→    ceq r0, z
   214→    brt halt_loop
   215→    push r1
   216→    push r0
   217→    la r2, -65280
   218→uart_puts_final_tx:
   219→    lb r0, 1(r2)
   220→    cls r0, z
   221→    brt uart_puts_final_tx
   222→    pop r0
   223→    sb r0, 0(r2)
   224→    pop r1
   225→    add r1, 1
   226→    bra uart_puts_final_loop
   227→
   228→halt_loop:
   229→    bra halt_loop
   230→
   231→; ============================================================
   232→; vm_trap — centralized trap handler
   233→; r0 = trap code (0-7). Sets status=2, trap_code=r0, returns to vm_loop.
   234→; ============================================================
   235→vm_trap:
   236→    push r0
   237→    la r0, vm_state
   238→    push r0
   239→    pop fp
   240→    lc r0, 2
   241→    sw r0, 21(fp)
   242→    pop r0
   243→    sw r0, 24(fp)
   244→    la r0, vm_loop
   245→    jmp (r0)
   246→
   247→; ============================================================
   248→; Opcode handlers
   249→; ============================================================
   250→

1→# pv24a -- P-Code VM for COR24
     2→
     3→A p-code virtual machine and p-code assembler (pasm), both written in COR24 assembly. pv24a provides a language-neutral stack-based execution substrate for compilers (Pascal, Lisp, BASIC, etc.) that emit p-code bytecode.
     4→
     5→Runs on the COR24 emulator ([cor24-rs](https://github.com/sw-embed/cor24-rs)) and eventually on FPGA hardware ([COR24-TB](https://makerlisp.com)).
     6→
     7→## Project Status
     8→
     9→The VM interpreter is **functional** -- 30+ opcodes implemented with trap handling, all verified with embedded test bytecode. The p-code assembler (pasm) lexer is complete.
    10→
    11→### What Works
    12→
    13→- Stack operations: push, push_s, dup, drop, swap, over
    14→- Arithmetic: add, sub, mul, div, mod, neg
    15→- Bitwise: and, or, xor, not, shl, shr
    16→- Comparison: eq, ne, lt, le, gt, ge (push 1/0)
    17→- Control flow: jmp, jz, jnz
    18→- Procedures: call, calln, ret, enter, leave
    19→- Local/argument access: loadl, storel, loada, storea
    20→- Global access: loadg, storeg, addrg, addrl
    21→- Nonlocal access: loadn, storen (static link chain traversal for nested Pascal procedures)
    22→- Indirect memory: load, store, loadb, storeb
    23→- System calls: HALT, PUTC, GETC, LED, ALLOC (bump allocator), FREE
    24→
    25→### What's Next
    26→
    27→- P-code assembler (pasm) -- parser, two-pass assembly (lexer done)
    28→- Test suite with golden output comparison
    29→- End-to-end: pasm source -> bytecode -> VM execution
    30→
    31→## Related
    32→
    33→- [tf24a](https://github.com/softwarewrighter/tf24a) -- DTC Forth in COR24 assembly
    34→- [tc24r](https://github.com/softwarewrighter/tc24r) -- Tiny C compiler for COR24 (Rust)
    35→- [tml24c](https://github.com/softwarewrighter/tml24c) -- Tiny Macro Lisp for COR24
    36→- [cor24-rs](https://github.com/sw-embed/cor24-rs) -- COR24 assembler and emulator (Rust)
    37→- [COR24-TB](https://makerlisp.com) -- The COR24 FPGA target board
    38→
    39→## Documentation
    40→
    41→| Document | Description |
    42→|----------|-------------|
    43→| [Design](docs/design.md) | VM spec: opcodes, encoding, call frame layout, pasm syntax |
    44→| [Architecture](docs/architecture.md) | System layers, memory map, register allocation |
    45→| [PRD](docs/prd.md) | Product requirements, deliverables, success criteria |
    46→| [Research](docs/research.txt) | Deep research on p-code VM design and implementation approach |
    47→
    48→## Building and Running
    49→
    50→Requires [cor24-rs](https://github.com/sw-embed/cor24-rs) (`cor24-run` binary).
    51→
    52→```bash
    53→# Assemble and run the VM with embedded test bytecode
    54→cor24-run --run pvm.s --speed 0
    55→
    56→# With UART input (for GETC testing)
    57→cor24-run --run pvm.s -u 'A' --speed 0 -n 5000000
    58→
    59→# Dump CPU state after halt
    60→cor24-run --run pvm.s --dump --speed 0
    61→
    62→# Trace last N instructions
    63→cor24-run --run pvm.s --trace 100 --speed 0
    64→
    65→# Run pasm lexer with source from UART (EOT = end of input)
    66→cor24-run --run pasm.s -u "$(printf '.proc main 0\npush 42\nhalt\n.end\n\x04')" --speed 0 -n 5000000
    67→```
    68→
    69→## COR24 Architecture at a Glance
    70→
    71→- **24-bit** registers and address space (16 MB)
    72→- **3 GPRs**: r0, r1, r2 (plus fp, sp, z)
    73→- **Variable-length** instruction encoding (1/2/4 bytes)
    74→- Hardware multiply, no hardware divide
    75→- Little-endian, byte-addressable
    76→- UART and LED via memory-mapped I/O

## Summary: PASM Assembly Syntax and Examples

Based on my thorough analysis of the pv24a project, here's what I found about pasm assembly:

### **1. PROCEDURE DEFINITIONS (.proc / .end)**

From `/Users/mike/github/sw-vibe-coding/pv24a/docs/design.md` (lines 186-209):

```
.proc main 0                 ; procedure, 0 locals
    push msg                  ; push address of msg
    call puts
    halt
.end

.proc puts 1                 ; 1 local (loop pointer)
    loada 0                   ; arg0 = string address
    storel 0                  ; local0 = current ptr
loop:
    loadl 0
    loadb                     ; load byte at ptr
    dup
    jz done                   ; if zero, done
    sys 1                     ; PUTC
    loadl 0
    push 1
    add
    storel 0                  ; ptr++
    jmp loop
done:
    drop                      ; drop the zero
    ret 1                     ; return, clean 1 arg
.end
```

**Key points:**
- `.proc NAME nlocals` — defines procedure with name and local slot count
- `.end` — terminates procedure
- Procedures are scoped (labels local to enclosing .proc/.end)

---

### **2. INSTRUCTION MNEMONICS**

**Complete list from `/Users/mike/github/sw-vibe-coding/pv24a/pasm.s` (lines 2148-2248):**

#### Stack Operations (0x00-0x06):
- `halt` — stop VM (opcode 0x00, no operand)
- `push imm24` — push 24-bit immediate (0x01, 4 bytes)
- `push_s imm8` — push sign-extended 8-bit (0x02, 2 bytes)
- `dup` — duplicate top (0x03)
- `drop` — discard top (0x04)
- `swap` — swap top two (0x05)
- `over` — copy second to top (0x06)

#### Arithmetic/Logic (0x10-0x1B):
- `add`, `sub`, `mul` — basic arithmetic
- `div` — divide (traps on zero)
- `mod` — modulo (traps on zero)
- `neg` — negate
- `and`, `or`, `xor`, `not` — bitwise ops
- `shl`, `shr` — shifts

#### Comparison (0x20-0x25, push 1/0):
- `eq`, `ne`, `lt`, `le`, `gt`, `ge`

#### Control Flow (0x30-0x36):
- `jmp addr24` — unconditional jump (3-byte operand)
- `jz addr24` — jump if zero (pops flag)
- `jnz addr24` — jump if nonzero
- `call addr24` — call procedure
- `ret nargs8` — return, clean args (1-byte operand)
- `calln depth8 addr24` — call with static link (nested procedures)
- `trap code8` — trigger trap

#### Frame Management (0x40-0x4B):
- `enter nlocals8` — setup frame, reserve locals
- `leave` — teardown frame
- `loadl off8`, `storel off8` — local variables
- `loadg off24`, `storeg off24` — global variables
- `addrl off8`, `addrg off24` — address-of operations
- `loada idx8`, `storea idx8` — argument access
- `loadn depth8 off8`, `storen depth8 off8` — nonlocal (nested procs)

#### Indirect Memory (0x50-0x53):
- `load` — load word from address
- `store` — store word to address
- `loadb` — load byte (zero-extended)
- `storeb` — store byte

#### System (0x60):
- `sys id8` — system call (ID: 0=HALT, 1=PUTC, 2=GETC, 3=LED, 4=ALLOC, 5=FREE)

---

### **3. HOW LABELS WORK**

From `/Users/mike/github/sw-vibe-coding/pv24a/docs/design.md` (lines 222-232):

```
loop:
    ; instructions...
    jmp loop
```

**Key points:**
- Labels end with `:` on their own line
- Local to enclosing `.proc` / `.end` block
- Resolved in pass 1 of two-pass assembler
- Can be targets for `jmp`, `jz`, `jnz`

---

### **4. MNEMONIC TABLE (from pasm.s)**

The assembler uses this table to lookup opcode/operand type (lines 2148-2248):

```
"halt" → opcode=0x00, type=NONE
"push" → opcode=0x01, type=IMM24
"push_s" → opcode=0x02, type=IMM8
"dup" → opcode=0x03, type=NONE
"div" → opcode=0x13, type=NONE
"mod" → opcode=0x14, type=NONE
"load" → opcode=0x50, type=NONE
"store" → opcode=0x51, type=NONE
"sys" → opcode=0x60, type=IMM8
"jz" → opcode=0x31, type=IMM24
"jmp" → opcode=0x30, type=IMM24
"call" → opcode=0x33, type=IMM24
"enter" → opcode=0x40, type=IMM8
"ret" → opcode=0x34, type=IMM8
```

Operand types:
- `NONE` — 1-byte instruction only
- `IMM8` — 2-byte total (opcode + 1 byte)
- `IMM24` — 4-byte total (opcode + 3 bytes)
- `D8_A24` — 5-byte (calln depth + address)
- `D8_O8` — 3-byte (loadn/storen depth + offset)

---

### **5. INTEGER-TO-STRING CONVERSION EXAMPLE**

From `/Users/mike/github/sw-vibe-coding/pv24a/pasm.s` (lines 2040-2138):

**print_number procedure** — converts and prints a signed integer:

```
print_number:
    ; Handle negative: print '-', negate
    ; Store value in num_val
    lc r0, 0
    push r0              ; Push sentinel (0)
    
    ; Extract digits loop
pnum_extract:
    lw r0, 0(r2)        ; Load num_val
    ceq r0, z
    brt pnum_output
    ; Call div10: divides num_val by 10
    jal r1, (r2)
    add r0, 48          ; Convert remainder to ASCII ('0'+digit)
    push r0             ; Stack digits in reverse
    bra pnum_extract
    
    ; Pop and print digits until sentinel
pnum_output:
    pop r0
    ceq r0, z
    brt pnum_ret
    jal r1, uart_putc   ; Print digit
    bra pnum_output
```

**div10 helper** (lines 2107-2138):

```
div10:
    ; Divide num_val by 10 using repeated subtraction
    lw r0, 0(r2)        ; Load dividend
    lc r2, 0
    ; Loop: r0 -= 10 until r0 < 10
d10_loop:
    clu r0, r2          ; r2=10, unsigned compare
    brt d10_done        ; if r0 < 10, done
    add r0, -10
    ; Increment quotient
    add r0, 1
    bra d10_loop
d10_done:
    ; r0 = remainder, store quotient to num_val
    sw r0, 0(r2)
    jmp (r1)
```

**Key pattern:**
1. Extract digits via repeated division by 10
2. Stack each digit (reversed) with sentinel (0) at bottom
3. Pop and print until sentinel reached
4. Uses `div10` helper that divides via repeated subtraction (no hardware divide in COR24)

---

### **6. COMPLETE EXAMPLE: SIMPLE PROGRAM WITH PROCEDURE CALL**

From design.md documentation (lines 186-209):

```
.data msg 72, 101, 108, 108, 111, 0   ; "Hello\0" as bytes

.proc main 0                 ; 0 locals
    push msg                  ; Push string address
    call puts                 ; Call puts
    halt
.end

.proc puts 1                 ; 1 local (current pointer)
    loada 0                   ; Load arg[0] = string address
    storel 0                  ; local[0] = ptr
loop:
    loadl 0                   ; Load local[0] (ptr)
    loadb                     ; Load byte at address
    dup                       ; Duplicate for both jz test and putc
    jz done                   ; Jump if null terminator
    sys 1                     ; PUTC — print character
    loadl 0
    push 1
    add                       ; Increment pointer
    storel 0                  ; Store back to local[0]
    jmp loop                  ; Loop
done:
    drop                      ; Discard the null byte
    ret 1                     ; Return, clean 1 arg
.end
```

**Call frame layout** (from design.md lines 128-143):

```
         ┌──────────────────┐  ← new csp (after enter)
         │ local[N-1]       │  offset = +(N-1)*3 from fp
         │ ...              │
         │ local[0]         │  offset = 0 from fp
fp ───→  ├──────────────────┤
         │ procedure ID     │  offset = -3
         │ static link      │  offset = -6  (nested procs)
         │ dynamic link     │  offset = -9  (caller's fp)
         │ return PC        │  offset = -12
         ├──────────────────┤
         │ arg[N-1]         │  offset = -15  (from fp)
         │ ...              │
         │ arg[0]           │  offset = -15 - (N-1)*3
         └──────────────────┘
```

---

### **7. TWO-PASS ASSEMBLER ARCHITECTURE**

From pasm.s and design.md:

**Pass 1:**
- Collect labels, procedure addresses, symbol table
- Count code/data/global sizes
- No code generation

**Pass 2:**
- Emit bytecode with resolved symbol references
- Generate machine-readable .p24 files

**Key data structures:**
- Symbol table: name pool, type (CONST/LABEL/PROC/GLOBAL/DATA), value
- Mnemonic table: string → (opcode, operand type)
- Directives: `.const`, `.global`, `.data`, `.proc`, `.end`

---

### **FILE PATHS**

- **Specification & Examples:** `/Users/mike/github/sw-vibe-coding/pv24a/docs/design.md`
- **Assembler Implementation:** `/Users/mike/github/sw-vibe-coding/pv24a/pasm.s` (2543 lines)
- **VM Implementation:** `/Users/mike/github/sw-vibe-coding/pv24a/pvm.s` (2050+ lines)
- **README:** `/Users/mike/github/sw-vibe-coding/pv24a/README.md`

    lw r0, 0(r2)        ; Load num_val
    ceq r0, z
    brt pnum_output
    ; Call div10: divides num_val by 10
    jal r1, (r2)
    add r0, 48          ; Convert remainder to ASCII ('0'+digit)
    push r0             ; Stack digits in reverse
    bra pnum_extract
    
    ; Pop and print digits until sentinel
pnum_output:
    pop r0
    ceq r0, z
    brt pnum_ret
    jal r1, uart_putc   ; Print digit
    bra pnum_output
```

**div10 helper** (lines 2107-2138):

```
div10:
    ; Divide num_val by 10 using repeated subtraction
    lw r0, 0(r2)        ; Load dividend
    lc r2, 0
    ; Loop: r0 -= 10 until r0 < 10
d10_loop:
    clu r0, r2          ; r2=10, unsigned compare
    brt d10_done        ; if r0 < 10, done
    add r0, -10
    ; Increment quotient
    add r0, 1
    bra d10_loop
d10_done:
    ; r0 = remainder, store quotient to num_val
    sw r0, 0(r2)
    jmp (r1)
```

**Key pattern:**
1. Extract digits via repeated division by 10
2. Stack each digit (reversed) with sentinel (0) at bottom
3. Pop and print until sentinel reached
4. Uses `div10` helper that divides via repeated subtraction (no hardware divide in COR24)

---

### **6. COMPLETE EXAMPLE: SIMPLE PROGRAM WITH PROCEDURE CALL**

From design.md documentation (lines 186-209):

```
.data msg 72, 101, 108, 108, 111, 0   ; "Hello\0" as bytes

.proc main 0                 ; 0 locals
    push msg                  ; Push string address
    call puts                 ; Call puts
    halt
.end

.proc puts 1                 ; 1 local (current pointer)
    loada 0                   ; Load arg[0] = string address
    storel 0                  ; local[0] = ptr
loop:
    loadl 0                   ; Load local[0] (ptr)
    loadb                     ; Load byte at address
    dup                       ; Duplicate for both jz test and putc
    jz done                   ; Jump if null terminator
    sys 1                     ; PUTC — print character
    loadl 0
    push 1
    add                       ; Increment pointer
    storel 0                  ; Store back to local[0]
    jmp loop                  ; Loop
done:
    drop                      ; Discard the null byte
    ret 1                     ; Return, clean 1 arg
.end
```

**Call frame layout** (from design.md lines 128-143):

```
         ┌──────────────────┐  ← new csp (after enter)
         │ local[N-1]       │  offset = +(N-1)*3 from fp
         │ ...              │
         │ local[0]         │  offset = 0 from fp
fp ───→  ├──────────────────┤
         │ procedure ID     │  offset = -3
         │ static link      │  offset = -6  (nested procs)
         │ dynamic link     │  offset = -9  (caller's fp)
         │ return PC        │  offset = -12
         ├──────────────────┤
         │ arg[N-1]         │  offset = -15  (from fp)
         │ ...              │
         │ arg[0]           │  offset = -15 - (N-1)*3
         └──────────────────┘
```

---

### **7. TWO-PASS ASSEMBLER ARCHITECTURE**

From pasm.s and design.md:

**Pass 1:**
- Collect labels, procedure addresses, symbol table
- Count code/data/global sizes
- No code generation

**Pass 2:**
- Emit bytecode with resolved symbol references
- Generate machine-readable .p24 files

**Key data structures:**
- Symbol table: name pool, type (CONST/LABEL/PROC/GLOBAL/DATA), value
- Mnemonic table: string → (opcode, operand type)
- Directives: `.const`, `.global`, `.data`, `.proc`, `.end`

; _p24p_write_int ( n -- )
; Print signed integer to UART as decimal.
; Handles negative numbers (prints '-' prefix).
; Uses div/mod by 10 to extract digits, pushes onto eval stack
; in reverse order with a zero sentinel, then prints from most
; significant to least significant via sys 1 (PUTC).
.proc _p24p_write_int 1
    enter 1
    loada 0              ; load argument n
    ; check for negative
    dup                  ; n n
    push 0               ; n n 0
    lt                   ; n (n<0?)
    jz positive
    ; print minus sign
    push 45              ; n '-'
    sys 1                ; n
    neg                  ; -n
positive:
    storel 0             ; local0 = abs(n)
    push 0               ; push sentinel
extract:
    loadl 0              ; load n
    push 10
    mod                  ; n % 10
    push 48
    add                  ; n % 10 + '0' = digit char
    loadl 0
    push 10
    div                  ; n / 10
    storel 0             ; update n = n / 10
    loadl 0
    jnz extract          ; if n != 0, extract more digits
print:
    dup
    jz done
    sys 1                ; PUTC digit
    jmp print
done:
    drop                 ; discard sentinel
    leave
    ret 1
.end

# pv24a -- P-Code VM for COR24

A p-code virtual machine and p-code assembler (pasm), both written in COR24 assembly. pv24a provides a language-neutral stack-based execution substrate for compilers (Pascal, Lisp, BASIC, etc.) that emit p-code bytecode.

Runs on the COR24 emulator ([cor24-rs](https://github.com/sw-embed/cor24-rs)) and eventually on FPGA hardware ([COR24-TB](https://makerlisp.com)).

## Project Status

The VM interpreter is **functional** -- 30+ opcodes implemented with trap handling, all verified with embedded test bytecode. The p-code assembler (pasm) lexer is complete.

### What Works

- Stack operations: push, push_s, dup, drop, swap, over
- Arithmetic: add, sub, mul, div, mod, neg
- Bitwise: and, or, xor, not, shl, shr
- Comparison: eq, ne, lt, le, gt, ge (push 1/0)
- Control flow: jmp, jz, jnz
- Procedures: call, calln, ret, enter, leave
- Local/argument access: loadl, storel, loada, storea
- Global access: loadg, storeg, addrg, addrl
- Nonlocal access: loadn, storen (static link chain traversal for nested Pascal procedures)
- Indirect memory: load, store, loadb, storeb
- System calls: HALT, PUTC, GETC, LED, ALLOC (bump allocator), FREE

### What's Next

- P-code assembler (pasm) -- parser, two-pass assembly (lexer done)
- Test suite with golden output comparison
- End-to-end: pasm source -> bytecode -> VM execution

content.content:
; pvmasm.s — P-Code Assembler + VM for COR24
;
; Integrated assembler and virtual machine:
;   1. Read .spc source from UART
;   2. Two-pass assembly to bytecode (code+data contiguous in code_buf)
;   3. Execute assembled bytecode in the p-code VM
;
; Assembly phases:
;   Pass 1: collect symbols (labels, procs, consts, globals, data), compute sizes
;   Pass 2: emit bytecode, resolve symbol references
;
; Token types:
;   0 = TOK_EOF     end of input
;   1 = TOK_NL      newline
;   2 = TOK_NUM     integer literal (value in tok_value)
;   3 = TOK_IDENT   identifier/mnemonic/name (string in tok_buf)
;   4 = TOK_DIR     directive without '.' (string in tok_buf)
;   5 = TOK_LABEL   label definition without ':' (string in tok_buf)
;   6 = TOK_COMMA   comma separator
;
; Symbol types:
;   0 = SYM_CONST   named constant (value = literal)
;   1 = SYM_LABEL   code label (value = code offset)
;   2 = SYM_PROC    procedure entry (value = code offset)
;   3 = SYM_GLOBAL  global variable (value = global segment offset)
;   4 = SYM_DATA    data block (value = data segment offset)
;
; Operand types (mnemonic table):
;   0 = NONE    1-byte instruction
;   1 = IMM8    2-byte: opcode + byte
;   2 = IMM24   4-byte: opcode + word
;   3 = D8_A24  5-byte: opcode + byte + word (calln)
;   4 = D8_O8   3-byte: opcode + byte + byte (loadn/storen)
;
; Register allocation:
;   r0 = work/scratch, parameter, return value
;   r1 = return address (jal) or scratch
;   r2 = scratch, function address for jal
;   fp = memory base for indexed loads/stores
;   sp = COR24 hardware stack (EBR)
;
; UART: data at -65280 (0xFF0100), status at -65279 (0xFF0101)
;   TX busy = status bit 7 (sign bit via lb sign-extend)
;   RX ready = status bit 0

; ============================================================
; Entry point
; ============================================================
_start:
    ; Print boot message

file.content:
; pr24p — Pascal Runtime Library
; Phase 0: Hand-written .spc stubs for p-code VM syscall wrappers

; _p24p_write_int ( n -- )
; Print signed integer to UART as decimal.
; Handles negative numbers (prints '-' prefix).
; Uses div/mod by 10 to extract digits, pushes onto eval stack
; in reverse order with a zero sentinel, then prints from most
; significant to least significant via sys 1 (PUTC).
.proc _p24p_write_int 1
    enter 1
    loada 0              ; load argument n
    ; check for negative
    dup                  ; n n
    push 0               ; n n 0
    lt                   ; n (n<0?)
    jz positive
    ; print minus sign
    push 45              ; n '-'
    sys 1                ; n
    neg                  ; -n
positive:
    storel 0             ; local0 = abs(n)
    push 0               ; push sentinel
extract:
    loadl 0              ; load n
    push 10
    mod                  ; n % 10
    push 48
    add                  ; n % 10 + '0' = digit char
    loadl 0
    push 10
    div                  ; n / 10
    storel 0             ; update n = n / 10
    loadl 0
    jnz extract          ; if n != 0, extract more digits
print:
    dup
    jz done
    sys 1                ; PUTC digit
    jmp print
done:
    drop                 ; discard sentinel
    leave
    ret 1
.end

content.text:
Good — I've confirmed the VM semantics:
- `call` saves frame header (return PC, dynamic link, static link, saved ESP) on call stack
- `enter N` sets fp_vm = csp, reserves N locals — **required** for `loadl`/`storel`/`loada` to work
- `leave` discards locals (csp = fp_vm)  
- `ret N` restores frame, cleans N args from eval stack

My code correctly uses `enter 1` / `leave` / `ret 1`. The `hello.spc` example omits `enter` but that appears to be an oversight — without it, the frame pointer isn't set up.

Now let me commit and complete the step.
